items.title: Ypsilanti Township confronts U-M facility on 144 acres amid water moratorium bid
items.dek: University of Michigan and Los Alamos National Laboratory control 144 acres at Textile and Bridge roads for a high-performance computing site. The township, which receives no property taxes from the project, has already demanded a 12-month pause on water and sewer hookups for such facilities. Residents 30 minutes away stand to lose local water capacity and face unmitigated infrastructure strain without new safeguards.
items.findings: U-M bought 20 acres at 10221 Textile Rd for $1.5 million in 2024 and over 100 acres at 10455/10635 Textile Rd for $5.2 million in 2026, bringing total control to roughly 144 acres | Resolution 2026-08, passed April 15, 2026, asks YCUA for a 12-month moratorium on water and sewer services to hyperscale and AI facilities citing up to 5 million gallons daily demand and wastewater treatment needs | Township Resolution 2026-05 unanimously opposed any Los Alamos nuclear research facility siting in Ypsilanti Township | U-M is exempt from township zoning and property taxes as a state university | YCUA voted April 22 in favor of a 12-month moratorium of delivering, committing, reserving or approving water and sewer services to data centers and AI computing facilities | The July 15 informational ses
items.perspective: The board unanimously backed Resolutions 2026-05 and 2026-08 in special meetings this year. Tomorrow's session gives residents a direct forum to question project leaders on water, power, and security impacts.

documents.label: Letter from U of M President Dominico Grasso and LANL Director Thom Ma

items.judge_note: Document-based agenda preview revealing U-M/Los Alamos 144-acre site and tax-exempt status amid water moratorium is incremental but real.

items.title: Coldwater Starts Data Center Ordinance Work; Girard Adopts Zoning Rules
items.dek: Both communities report no local data center projects while choosing permanent zoning standards over temporary halts.
items.judge_note: Two Michigan communities adopting permanent data center zoning standards is concrete local action, even without active projects yet.
